chunk_summary: Milk-triggered FPIES is a non-IgE-mediated CMPA phenotype requiring supervised diagnosis and management, including tailored nutrition strategies and supervised oral food challenges (OFCs).
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria for milk-triggered FPIES.
Learn appropriate acute and chronic management strategies for milk-triggered FPIES.
Identify safe nutritional alternatives and escalation protocols for formula-fed infants.
Plan supervised oral food challenges (OFCs) for tolerance assessment.
key_facts: Milk-triggered FPIES presents with repetitive vomiting 1–4 hours post-ingestion, pallor, and lethargy, but without urticaria or wheeze.
Severe reactions can include hypotension and hypothermia, requiring supportive care with hydration and antiemetics, not IM epinephrine.
Chronic FPIES in formula-fed infants may cause intermittent emesis, watery diarrhea, and failure to thrive, improving after trigger removal.
For formula-fed infants, start with extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) and escalate to amino acid formula (AAF) if severe reactions persist.
Tolerance assessment is typically conducted via supervised OFC around 12 months after the last reaction, with individualized timing.
reference_quote: The classic presentation is repetitive vomiting 1–4 hours after cow’s milk ingestion with pallor and lethargy but without urticaria or wheeze; severe reactions can include hypotension and hypothermia. (AAAAI, 2017)
document_key_topics: Acute and chronic management of FPIES
